nativeRange.habitat: Hard-bottom reef habitats; typically found attached to limestone, rubble, or rocky substrate in high-current zones

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that may graze heavily on macroalgae

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo showing iridescent branching thallus with blue structural coloration under reef lighting.

summary: A compact Caribbean red macroalgae with a striking structural iridescence — the blue-green shimmer is produced by light refraction off the thallus surface, not pigment. Generally suited to display placement in established reef tanks.
careNotes:
Color-Shifting Laurencia (Laurencia iridescens, trade name; accepted scientific name Yuzurua iridescens Sentíes & M.J.Wynne 2015, family Rhodomelaceae, order Ceramiales) is a specialty red macroalgae collected from Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico hard-bottom habitats. Its most distinctive trait is a brilliant blue-to-green iridescence that appears under reef lighting — this coloration is structural in origin, produced by light interference and refraction off the microstructure of the thallus surface rather than by photosynthetic or accessory pigments. Specimens may arrive looking duller after shipping stress but generally tend to recover their iridescent glow after a few days under stable reef lighting.

The thallus forms compact, textured clumps of rounded, club-shaped branchlets, typically reaching 4–8 cm in diameter. It has been documented growing attached to hard substrates (limestone, rubble, aragonite) and generally requires secure anchoring — keeping the holdfast off the substrate surface tends to support survival. Moderate to high, turbulent flow has been reported to improve growth and longevity; stagnant conditions are best avoided.

Mosaic Macros is currently the primary hobby source for this species; it is not commonly stocked by mainstream retailers. Care extrapolation draws on family-level (Rhodomelaceae) husbandry knowledge for ornamental red macroalgae. The species is considered intermediate in care level — less forgiving than refugium staples like Chaetomorpha, but not as demanding as calcareous greens. It has been noted as seasonal and limited in availability, typically hand-collected in late spring to summer.

Nutrient uptake is generally considered low compared to refugium-grade macroalgae; it is kept primarily as an ornamental display specimen rather than a nitrate/phosphate exporter. Propagation in the aquarium is typically achieved by fragmentation — separating a portion of the thallus with an intact holdfast base. Per Mosaic care guide guidance: as a red or brown alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand — attach to rock via superglue, zip-tie/fishing line, or wedging instead. (Sand-bed acceptance per Mosaic is limited to Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea genera, plus some calcified Dasycladales.)

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary and sole specialty hobby source for this species. Product page confirms structural iridescence, branching morphology, and Laurencia iridescens scientific name.

additional.name: Gulf Coast Ecosystems (Iridescent Candy Algae)
additional.notes: Secondary hobby source. Cross-checked sizing (1.5–3 in = ~4–8 cm), lighting (moderate), flow (moderate-high), structural color description, and Gulf/Atlantic native range.

additional.name: TankFacts – Laurencia iridescens
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), salinity 1.023–1.026 SG, pH 7.8–8.4. Partial entry but confirmed key parameters.

additional.name: WoRMS – Laurencia iridescens (AphiaID 496287)
additional.notes: Confirmed family Rhodomelaceae, order Ceramiales. Notes accepted name is Yuzurua iridescens (Sentíes & M.J.Wynne, 2015); Laurencia iridescens is the original basionym used in the aquarium trade.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
